questions.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

answer.oneLiner: Authentication proves who you are. Authorization decides what you are allowed to do. They are two separate steps and both must happen in the right order.
answer.detail: Authentication happens first. The user sends credentials - a password, a token, a certificate - and the system confirms their identity. Think of it as checking someone's ID card at the door. Authorization happens second, after identity is confirmed. The system checks what actions that specific identity is permitted to perform. In ASP.NET Core, UseAuthentication() must be registered before UseAuthorization() in the middleware pipeline. If authentication fails, the server returns a 401 Unauthorized. If authentication succeeds but the user lacks permission, the server returns a 403 Forbidden. Mixing up these two concepts is one of the most common junior mistakes in interviews and in real code.
keyPoints: Authentication = confirming identity (who you are), returns 401 if it fails
Authorization = confirming permissions (what you can do), returns 403 if it fails
UseAuthentication() must come before UseAuthorization() in the middleware pipeline
A valid token proves identity but does not guarantee access to every resource

code.note: Reversing the middleware order causes authorization to run with no identity, so every protected endpoint gets a 401 even for valid users.

answer.analogy: Authentication opens the door; authorization tells you which rooms you can enter.
redFlags: Saying 'authentication and authorization are basically the same thing'
Not knowing which HTTP status code each failure returns (401 vs 403)
Confusing the middleware registration order in ASP.NET Core
Saying 'if you have a valid JWT you can access anything'

followUpsQA.q: Which HTTP status code does each failure return?
followUpsQA.a: 401 Unauthorized means authentication failed - the caller has no valid identity. 403 Forbidden means authentication succeeded but authorization failed - the identity lacks permission.

followUpsQA.q: Why must UseAuthentication come before UseAuthorization?
followUpsQA.a: Authorization evaluates the current user's claims. If authentication has not run yet, there is no user identity to evaluate, so every check fails regardless of the token.

followUpsQA.q: Can you authorize without authenticating first?
followUpsQA.a: No. You need a confirmed identity to evaluate permissions against. Anonymous access is a special case where the system explicitly allows the absence of identity.

followUpsQA.q: Give a real-world example of each.
followUpsQA.a: Authentication: a customer logging into an e-commerce site with email and password. Authorization: only users with the 'OrderManager' role can process refunds.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if a user with a valid JWT hits an admin-only endpoint?
followUpsQA.a: Authentication passes and the identity is established, but authorization fails and returns 403 because the user's claims do not satisfy the admin role requirement.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between authentication and authorization?

Authentication proves who you are - the system validates your credentials and establishes your identity. Authorization happens after that and decides what you are allowed to do based on that identity. They are always two separate steps.
How does this play out in ASP.NET Core specifically?

In the middleware pipeline, UseAuthentication() must come before UseAuthorization(). If you reverse the order, the authz middleware runs before the user identity is set, so every protected endpoint returns 401 even for valid tokens. The status codes matter too - authentication failure is 401, authorization failure is 403.
A user sends a valid JWT to an admin-only endpoint. Walk me through what happens.

Authentication middleware validates the JWT signature, expiry, issuer, and audience - it passes. The ClaimsPrincipal is populated with the user's claims. Authorization middleware then checks whether those claims satisfy the admin policy. If the user does not have the Admin role claim, it returns 403 Forbidden - not 401, because the identity is known.
Why does the distinction matter in practice, not just theory?

Security logging, incident response, and client error handling all depend on knowing which step failed. A 401 means 'send credentials or re-authenticate.' A 403 means 'you are known but not permitted - do not retry with the same token.' Mixing them up confuses clients and hides real access control failures in logs.
Can you authorize an anonymous user?

Anonymous access is a special case where the system explicitly permits the absence of identity - for example, a public product catalog endpoint. It is not really authorizing without authenticating; it is granting access with no identity requirement. The [AllowAnonymous] attribute in ASP.NET Core does exactly this, bypassing both steps.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What's the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect?

answer.oneLiner: OAuth 2.0 is a framework for delegating API access using access tokens. OpenID Connect is an identity layer built on top of OAuth that adds an ID token so you know who the user is.
answer.detail: OAuth 2.0 was designed to answer one question: can this application access this resource on behalf of a user? It issues an access token that authorizes API calls. OAuth says nothing about who the user is - the access token only proves permission, not identity. OpenID Connect (OIDC) extends OAuth with a second token called the ID token. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims like sub (a unique user identifier), email, and name. OIDC also defines a userinfo endpoint for fetching additional claims. When you add the 'openid' scope to an OAuth flow, the authorization server returns both an access token and an ID token. A classic mistake is using the access token to identify the user - that token may be opaque, may not contain user claims, or may be intended for a different audience. The ID token is the correct identity document.
keyPoints: OAuth 2.0 = authorization framework, issues access tokens for API access only
OIDC = identity layer on OAuth, adds an ID token (JWT) to authenticate who the user is
ID token contains identity claims: sub, email, name - access token does not guarantee these
Add the 'openid' scope to an OAuth flow to trigger OIDC and receive an ID token

code.note: The access token authorizes API calls; the ID token identifies the user. They are not interchangeable.

answer.analogy: OAuth is your car key that opens the garage; OIDC is your passport that proves who you are.
redFlags: Saying 'OAuth 2.0 is an authentication protocol' - it is an authorization framework
Using the access token to populate user profile data instead of the ID token
Confusing scopes (permissions) with claims (user attributes)
Not knowing what the 'openid' scope does

followUpsQA.q: What is in the ID token versus the access token?
followUpsQA.a: The ID token is a JWT with identity claims like sub, email, and name - it identifies the user. The access token grants permission to call an API and may be opaque or audience-specific.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use OAuth without OIDC?
followUpsQA.a: When you need delegated API access but do not need to know who the user is - for example, a server-to-server integration using the Client Credentials flow.

followUpsQA.q: What scope triggers OIDC behavior?
followUpsQA.a: Adding the 'openid' scope to the OAuth request tells the authorization server to include an ID token in the response alongside the access token.

followUpsQA.q: What is the userinfo endpoint?
followUpsQA.a: An OIDC endpoint protected by the access token that returns additional user claims - used when all needed claims do not fit in the ID token.

followUpsQA.q: Is it safe to use the access token as proof of identity?
followUpsQA.a: No. The access token may be opaque, may not contain user claims, and is intended for the resource server, not the client. Use the ID token or HttpContext.User.Claims instead.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect?

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework - it lets a user delegate API access to an application by issuing an access token. It does not tell you who the user is. OpenID Connect is an identity layer built on top of OAuth that adds an ID token, which is a JWT containing user identity claims like sub and email.
So what exactly is in each token?

The access token proves permission to call an API - it may be opaque or audience-specific and does not reliably contain user claims. The ID token is always a signed JWT with identity claims: at minimum 'sub' as the unique user identifier, and optionally email and name if you request those scopes.
A junior developer is reading the user's name from the access token to show in the UI. What would you tell them?

That is a bug waiting to happen. The access token is for the API, not the client - its format and claims are not guaranteed. The ID token or the claims populated by the OIDC middleware on HttpContext.User are the right source for user identity in the UI.
When would you use OAuth without OIDC?

For machine-to-machine calls using the Client Credentials flow - a background service calling a billing API does not have a user to identify, so there is no ID token and no need for OIDC. You just need an access token scoped to the API.
How do you trigger OIDC behavior in an OAuth flow?

By including 'openid' in the scope parameter of the authorization request. That signals the authorization server to include an ID token. You can also add 'email' and 'profile' scopes to get more claims in the ID token or available via the userinfo endpoint.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the PKCE flow and why is it used for SPAs and mobile apps?

answer.oneLiner: PKCE is the Authorization Code flow extended with a one-time secret called a code verifier, so public clients that cannot store a client secret can still safely exchange authorization codes.
answer.detail: The Authorization Code flow traditionally requires a client secret to exchange the authorization code for tokens. Public clients - SPAs, mobile apps, desktop apps - cannot keep a secret because their code is accessible to users. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) solves this by replacing the static client secret with a per-request dynamic proof. Before starting the flow, the client generates a random code_verifier string. It hashes that string (SHA-256) to create a code_challenge, which it sends with the authorization request. The authorization server stores the challenge. When the client later exchanges the authorization code for tokens, it sends the original code_verifier. The server hashes it and compares it to the stored challenge. An attacker who intercepts the authorization code cannot exchange it because they never had the code_verifier. This is why the OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice and RFC 8252 require PKCE for public clients, and why the implicit flow (which returned tokens directly in the URL without any proof) was deprecated.
keyPoints: PKCE replaces the client secret with a per-request code_verifier / code_challenge pair
code_challenge = SHA-256 hash of code_verifier, sent with the auth request
code_verifier is sent at token exchange - an intercepted code cannot be redeemed without it
Required for public clients (SPAs, mobile, native apps) and recommended for all clients today

code.note: Setting UsePkce = true makes the middleware generate the code_verifier and code_challenge automatically on each authorization request.

answer.analogy: PKCE is a one-time padlock - you set it before the journey and only you have the key to open it at the end.
redFlags: Still recommending the implicit flow for SPAs in 2024
Saying you can embed a client secret in a SPA or mobile app bundle
Not knowing what the code_verifier is or where it is stored during the flow
Confusing PKCE with mTLS or token binding

followUpsQA.q: Why was the implicit flow deprecated?
followUpsQA.a: The implicit flow returned tokens directly in the URL fragment with no proof of possession. Tokens were visible in browser history and referrer headers, and could be intercepted or replayed with no defense.

followUpsQA.q: Where is the code_verifier stored during the flow?
followUpsQA.a: In the client's memory or session for the duration of the flow only. It is generated fresh for each authorization request and discarded after the token exchange completes.

followUpsQA.q: What stops an attacker who intercepts the authorization code?
followUpsQA.a: They cannot exchange it because the authorization server requires the code_verifier that only the legitimate client generated and held. The server verifies it by hashing and comparing to the stored code_challenge.

followUpsQA.q: Does PKCE work alongside a client secret?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. Confidential clients can use both. RFC 9700 now recommends PKCE for all OAuth clients, not just public ones, as an extra layer of defense against authorization code interception attacks.

followUpsQA.q: What hash algorithm does PKCE use for the challenge?
followUpsQA.a: SHA-256, referred to as S256 in the OAuth spec. The plain method (no hashing) must not be used in production because it removes the security benefit entirely.

dialogue.text: What is PKCE and why does it exist?

PKCE stands for Proof Key for Code Exchange. It extends the Authorization Code flow so that public clients - SPAs and mobile apps - can safely complete the flow without a client secret. It replaces the static secret with a dynamic per-request proof that can only be used by the client that started the flow.
Walk me through how the challenge and verifier actually work.

Before redirecting to the authorization server, the client generates a random code_verifier. It hashes that with SHA-256 to create a code_challenge and sends the challenge with the authorization request. The server stores it. When the client later exchanges the authorization code for tokens, it sends the original code_verifier. The server hashes it and compares - they must match or the exchange is rejected.
Why can't you just put a client secret in the SPA instead?

A SPA's JavaScript bundle is downloaded and executed in the browser - anyone with DevTools can extract a hardcoded secret in seconds. PKCE has no static secret to steal because the verifier is generated fresh per request and never stored beyond that one flow.
What was wrong with the implicit flow that PKCE replaced?

The implicit flow returned tokens directly in the URL fragment - visible in browser history, referrer headers, and server logs. There was no authorization code step and no proof of possession, so stolen fragments could be replayed. The OAuth Security BCP deprecated it entirely for this reason.
Is PKCE only for public clients, or should confidential clients use it too?

RFC 9700 recommends PKCE for all OAuth clients now, not just public ones. Even confidential clients with a client secret benefit from PKCE as defense-in-depth against authorization code interception. In ASP.NET Core, UsePkce is true by default in .NET 8 for the OIDC middleware.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Describe the structure of a JWT and how you validate one.

answer.oneLiner: A JWT is three base64url-encoded parts - header, payload, and signature - joined by dots. You validate by verifying the signature and checking the issuer, audience, and expiry claims before trusting anything in the payload.
answer.detail: JWT stands for JSON Web Token. It has three parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature. The header is a JSON object containing the token type (JWT) and the signing algorithm (e.g., RS256 or HS256). The payload is a JSON object containing claims - standard ones like iss (issuer), aud (audience), exp (expiry), sub (subject), and nbf (not before), plus any custom claims. Both header and payload are base64url-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone can decode and read them. The signature is created by the issuing server using its private key (asymmetric) or shared secret (symmetric). Validation means: (1) verify the signature against the issuer's public key from their JWKS endpoint, (2) check iss matches your expected issuer, (3) check aud includes your API, (4) check exp has not passed, (5) check nbf is not in the future. The classic attack is accepting alg: none, which has no signature - you must explicitly reject it. ASP.NET Core's AddJwtBearer does all of this if you set all four TokenValidationParameters flags to true.
keyPoints: Header (algorithm) + Payload (claims) + Signature - all base64url-encoded, payload is NOT encrypted
Never put secrets or passwords in the payload - anyone can decode it
Validate signature, iss, aud, exp, and nbf - disabling any of these is a security vulnerability
Reject alg: none explicitly - an unsigned token is not a valid JWT

code.note: Disabling ValidateAudience lets tokens issued for another API be replayed against yours. Disabling ValidateLifetime lets expired tokens work forever.

answer.analogy: A JWT is a signed boarding pass - readable by anyone, but the signature proves the airline issued it.
redFlags: Saying the JWT payload is encrypted or private
Disabling ValidateAudience or ValidateLifetime to fix a validation error in staging
Not knowing what 'alg: none' means or why it is dangerous
Confusing signing (integrity) with encryption (confidentiality)

followUpsQA.q: Why can't you put a password or secret in the JWT payload?
followUpsQA.a: The payload is base64url-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone who intercepts the token can decode it with a single line of code and read every claim in plain text.

followUpsQA.q: What is JWKS and how is it used in validation?
followUpsQA.a: JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) is a public endpoint published by the identity provider that contains its public signing keys. The JWT bearer middleware fetches these keys and uses them to verify the token signature without needing any shared secret.

followUpsQA.q: What is the alg: none attack?
followUpsQA.a: An attacker sets the algorithm in the JWT header to 'none' and removes the signature. A vulnerable library that accepts this skips signature verification entirely, letting an attacker forge any claims they want.

followUpsQA.q: Why must you validate the audience (aud) claim?
followUpsQA.a: A token issued for another API could be replayed against your API. Validating that aud matches your API's identifier stops this token confusion attack.

followUpsQA.q: What claims does ASP.NET Core validate by default with AddJwtBearer?
followUpsQA.a: Signature, issuer, audience, and lifetime. All four TokenValidationParameters flags default to true, which is the correct secure default.

dialogue.text: Describe the structure of a JWT and how you validate one.

A JWT has three base64url-encoded parts separated by dots: a header with the algorithm, a payload with claims, and a signature. The payload is readable by anyone - it is encoded, not encrypted. Validation means verifying the signature against the issuer's public key, then checking that iss, aud, and exp all match what you expect.
A token arrives with the header set to alg: none. What do you do?

Reject it immediately. alg: none means no signature - any library that accepts it is skipping verification entirely. An attacker can forge any claims they want in an unsigned token. ASP.NET Core's AddJwtBearer rejects this by default, but you must not override that behavior.
A teammate turns off audience validation to stop a 401 error in staging. What is the risk?

That opens up a token confusion attack. Without audience validation, a token issued for the company's file storage API could be replayed against your customer data API. The aud claim exists precisely to bind a token to a specific audience - disabling it defeats that binding.
Where does the JWT bearer middleware get the signing keys to verify signatures?

From the identity provider's JWKS endpoint - a public URL that exposes the provider's public signing keys as JSON. The middleware fetches and caches these keys on startup and periodically refreshes them. This means the API never needs to hold a shared secret to verify RS256 tokens.
What should and should not go in the JWT payload?

Standard identity claims like sub, email, roles, and scopes belong there - anything the receiver needs to make a decision. Passwords, private keys, credit card numbers, or any sensitive data must never go in the payload because it is trivially decodable. If you need confidentiality for claims, use a JWE (JSON Web Encryption) token, not a standard JWT.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: JWT vs cookie authentication - when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Cookies suit server-rendered apps and are safer against XSS when HttpOnly, but need CSRF protection. JWTs suit APIs, SPAs, and cross-domain calls but need careful storage and a revocation strategy.
answer.detail: Cookie-based authentication stores a session identifier or encrypted token in a browser cookie. With HttpOnly and Secure flags, JavaScript cannot read the cookie, which protects it from XSS. The browser sends it automatically, which is convenient but also makes CSRF possible. Cookie auth is stateful - the server often tracks sessions in memory or a database. JWT-based authentication sends a signed token in the Authorization header. It is stateless - the server validates the token without looking up a session. This makes it natural for APIs, mobile clients, and cross-domain calls. The downsides are storage (localStorage is XSS-vulnerable), revocation difficulty, and token size. The best pattern for SPAs is a hybrid: a short-lived JWT access token sent in the Authorization header, plus a refresh token stored in an HttpOnly Secure cookie. This gives the statelessness of JWTs for API calls plus the XSS safety of an HttpOnly cookie for the credential that outlasts the access token.
keyPoints: Cookies with HttpOnly and Secure flags are safer against XSS but vulnerable to CSRF - use SameSite and antiforgery tokens
JWTs are stateless, good for APIs and cross-domain, but hard to revoke and need careful storage
Never store JWTs in localStorage in a security-sensitive app - use HttpOnly cookie or in-memory
The hybrid pattern (short JWT + refresh in HttpOnly cookie) gives the best of both

code.note: The refresh token is HttpOnly so XSS cannot steal it; the short-lived access token lives in JS memory only.

answer.analogy: Cookies are a hotel keycard the desk auto-renews; JWTs are a stamped day-pass you carry yourself.
redFlags: Storing JWTs in localStorage without mentioning the XSS risk
Not knowing that long-lived JWTs without revocation are a security risk
Saying cookies are always less secure than JWTs
Not knowing what SameSite and HttpOnly do to a cookie

followUpsQA.q: Why is localStorage risky for JWT storage?
followUpsQA.a: Any JavaScript on the page can read localStorage - including injected XSS scripts. A stolen JWT gives an attacker a working session until the token expires.

followUpsQA.q: What makes cookies vulnerable to CSRF?
followUpsQA.a: The browser automatically attaches cookies to every request to the matching domain, including ones triggered from a third-party site. An attacker can craft a form on their site that posts to your API and the browser sends the victim's cookie.

followUpsQA.q: What is the hybrid pattern and when would you use it?
followUpsQA.a: A short-lived JWT access token (5-15 minutes) kept in JS memory for API calls, plus a long-lived refresh token stored in an HttpOnly Secure cookie. Use it for SPAs that need stateless API access but also need the credential to survive a page refresh.

followUpsQA.q: What is the main downside of JWTs for auth?
followUpsQA.a: They are hard to revoke before expiry. Once issued, a JWT is valid until it expires unless you maintain a server-side denylist, which re-introduces statefulness.

followUpsQA.q: Which is better for a traditional server-rendered web app?
followUpsQA.a: Cookie-based session auth. The server controls the session, revocation is immediate (delete the session), and HttpOnly cookies are safe from XSS without any extra storage decisions.

dialogue.text: When would you choose JWT authentication over cookie-based, and vice versa?

I use cookies for server-rendered apps - the browser handles them automatically and with HttpOnly and Secure they are protected from XSS. I use JWTs for APIs, SPAs making cross-domain calls, and mobile apps where there is no browser to manage cookies. JWTs are stateless so the server does not track sessions.
A React SPA needs to store a JWT after login. Where does it go?

Not in localStorage - any XSS vulnerability on the page can steal it. The access token lives in JavaScript memory only. For persistence across page refreshes, I use a refresh token stored in an HttpOnly Secure cookie that the SPA cannot read but the server can - that is the hybrid pattern.
What is the tradeoff of making the JWT very long-lived to avoid the refresh complexity?

A long-lived JWT is a liability if it leaks - you cannot revoke it without a server-side denylist, which defeats statelessness. The standard answer is short-lived access tokens (5-15 minutes) plus a rotating refresh token. The refresh step is also where you can enforce revocation.
If you go with cookie auth, what do you add to handle CSRF?

Two things: set SameSite=Strict or Lax on the cookie to stop most cross-site requests at the browser level, and add antiforgery tokens for state-changing endpoints. In ASP.NET Core the [ValidateAntiForgeryToken] attribute or the automatic Razor Pages protection handles this. Bearer-token APIs are not CSRF-vulnerable because the browser does not auto-attach Authorization headers.
Which approach would you pick for an internal microservices API where all callers are other services?

JWTs with the OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow. There is no user browser involved, so cookies make no sense. Each service gets a client ID and secret, exchanges them for a short-lived access token from the identity server, and presents that JWT to the downstream API. The API validates the token statlessly with no session lookup needed.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do refresh tokens work and where should they be stored?

answer.oneLiner: A refresh token is a long-lived credential that the client exchanges for a new short-lived access token when the current one expires. Store it in an HttpOnly Secure cookie for web apps and rotate it on every use.
answer.detail: Access tokens are kept short-lived (5-15 minutes) to limit the damage if they leak, since they cannot easily be revoked. But requiring users to re-authenticate every 15 minutes is unacceptable. Refresh tokens solve this. When the access token expires, the client sends the refresh token to the authorization server's token endpoint. The server validates it, issues a new access token, and - if rotation is enabled - issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one. The old refresh token must never work again. Reuse detection is the defence against stolen refresh tokens: if the server sees a refresh token that was already used (rotated out), it knows the token family has been compromised and revokes all tokens for that user session. For storage: in web apps, use an HttpOnly Secure SameSite=Strict cookie so JavaScript cannot read it. Never use localStorage. On mobile, use platform secure storage (iOS Keychain, Android Keystore). The refresh token should also be stored hashed server-side (like a password) so a database breach does not expose live tokens.
keyPoints: Refresh tokens keep access tokens short-lived without forcing constant re-authentication
Rotate the refresh token on every use - the old one must immediately become invalid
Reuse detection: if an already-used token is presented again, revoke the whole token family
Store in HttpOnly Secure cookie (web) or platform secure storage (mobile) - never localStorage

code.note: Hashing the stored refresh token means a DB breach does not expose live tokens, the same way you hash passwords.

answer.analogy: A refresh token is like a hotel room key that gives you a new key each time you use it - and alerts the front desk if two people try to use the same key.
redFlags: Storing the refresh token in localStorage
Not rotating the refresh token on use, or not implementing reuse detection
Issuing refresh tokens that never expire
Storing refresh tokens in plain text in the database

followUpsQA.q: What is refresh token rotation?
followUpsQA.a: On every token refresh, the server issues a new refresh token and immediately invalidates the previous one. The client must use the newest token for the next refresh.

followUpsQA.q: How does reuse detection catch a stolen refresh token?
followUpsQA.a: If an attacker steals and uses a refresh token, rotation means the server issues a new one to the attacker. When the legitimate client then tries to use its copy (the now-rotated-out old one), the server detects reuse and revokes the entire token family for that session.

followUpsQA.q: Where should you store the refresh token server-side?
followUpsQA.a: Hashed, like a password. Store a SHA-256 hash of the token, not the raw token. A database breach then does not expose live credentials.

followUpsQA.q: How long should a refresh token live?
followUpsQA.a: It depends on the app's security profile. Banking apps might use 15-minute sessions with no refresh. Consumer SaaS typically uses days to weeks. Rotation means the token renews its own lifetime on each use.

followUpsQA.q: What is absolute versus sliding expiry for refresh tokens?
followUpsQA.a: Sliding expiry resets the expiry on each use, so an active user stays logged in. Absolute expiry forces re-authentication after a fixed period regardless of activity - better for high-security applications.

dialogue.text: Explain how refresh tokens work and where they should be stored.

Access tokens are kept short-lived to limit damage if they leak, but you cannot make users log in every 15 minutes. A refresh token is a long-lived credential the client exchanges for a new access token when the current one expires. It is a separate token, stored separately, and only ever sent to the token endpoint - never to the API itself.
What is rotation and why does it matter?

On every refresh call, the server issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one. This limits the window an attacker has to use a stolen token. The critical addition is reuse detection: if the server sees a refresh token that was already rotated out, it knows the family is compromised and revokes all tokens for that session.
A developer stores the refresh token in localStorage for simplicity. What is the problem?

Any JavaScript on the page can read localStorage - including XSS payloads. A stolen refresh token is far more dangerous than a stolen access token because it lasts much longer and can generate an unlimited number of access tokens. It belongs in an HttpOnly Secure SameSite=Strict cookie, which JavaScript cannot access.
How do you store refresh tokens on the server side?

Hashed, the same way you store passwords. Store a SHA-256 hash of the token in the database. When a refresh request comes in, hash the incoming token and compare. If the database is breached, the raw tokens are not exposed and cannot be replayed.
If you want to force a specific user to re-authenticate immediately, how do you do it?

Mark all their refresh token records as revoked in the database. On the next refresh attempt the server sees the revoked record and rejects it, returning 401. The short-lived access token will expire on its own within minutes. For a truly immediate kill you combine this with a security stamp check on the access token validation side.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Compare RBAC, claims-based, and policy-based authorization in ASP.NET Core.

answer.oneLiner: RBAC checks roles, claims-based checks specific attributes on the user, and policy-based combines both with custom logic into named reusable rules. Policy-based is the most flexible and is the right default for anything beyond simple role checks.
answer.detail: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is the simplest form. You decorate an endpoint with [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")] and ASP.NET Core checks whether the user's claims include a role claim with that value. It is coarse-grained and works well for simple admin/user distinctions. Claims-based authorization goes a step further. You check for a specific claim and optionally its value - for example, requiring a 'department' claim equal to 'Finance'. This is more precise than roles but still limited to single claim assertions. Policy-based authorization is the most powerful. You define named policies with AddAuthorization, combining claims requirements, role requirements, and custom IAuthorizationRequirement implementations. The real power comes from resource-based authorization - you pass the resource being accessed to IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync, and the handler can compare resource properties (like OwnerId) to the current user. This handles scenarios like 'a manager can only edit orders from their own branch' that no role or claim alone can express.
keyPoints: RBAC: [Authorize(Roles='Admin')] - coarse-grained, good for simple admin/user splits
Claims-based: RequireClaim('department', 'Finance') - more precise but limited to one claim assertion
Policy-based: named policies with custom IAuthorizationRequirement - flexible, centralised, testable
Resource-based authorization uses IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync to compare the resource to the user at runtime

code.note: Centralising the rule in a named policy means you update it in one place and every endpoint using it inherits the change.

answer.analogy: RBAC is a job title on a badge; claims are all the details on your ID card; policies are the written rules that say who can enter which room.
redFlags: Hardcoding role strings in every [Authorize] attribute across many controllers
Not knowing what IAuthorizationRequirement is used for
Confusing authentication claims with authorization policies
Saying RBAC is enough for all scenarios without mentioning resource-based authorization

followUpsQA.q: When would you write a custom IAuthorizationRequirement?
followUpsQA.a: When the rule cannot be expressed by a role or a single claim - for example, 'a sales rep can only edit leads assigned to them' requires comparing a resource property to the current user's ID at runtime.

followUpsQA.q: What is resource-based authorization?
followUpsQA.a: Passing the actual resource (a database record) to IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync so the handler can inspect both the resource and the user to make the access decision. You cannot express this with attributes alone.

followUpsQA.q: What is the default authorization policy in ASP.NET Core?
followUpsQA.a: You can configure a default policy in AddAuthorization. Setting it to RequireAuthenticatedUser makes every endpoint require authentication unless decorated with [AllowAnonymous] - this is the deny-by-default approach.

followUpsQA.q: How do you unit test a custom authorization requirement handler?
followUpsQA.a: Instantiate the handler directly, create an AuthorizationHandlerContext with a mock resource and ClaimsPrincipal, call HandleAsync, and assert on context.HasSucceeded or context.HasFailed.

followUpsQA.q: Are roles claims under the hood?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. In ASP.NET Core, roles are just a specific type of claim with the type ClaimTypes.Role. [Authorize(Roles='Admin')] is syntactic sugar for checking that claim.

dialogue.text: Compare RBAC, claims-based, and policy-based authorization in ASP.NET Core.

RBAC checks whether the user has a specific role claim - coarse-grained, good for simple admin/user splits. Claims-based checks for a specific claim and value. Policy-based is the most powerful: you define named policies that combine role and claim requirements plus custom handlers, and you reference the policy by name on the endpoint.
Why do you prefer policies over scattering [Authorize(Roles='...')] everywhere?

Because the rule lives in one place. If HR expands to include contractors, I update the 'CanViewSalaries' policy definition once and every endpoint using it automatically picks up the change. With hardcoded role strings, I have to find and update every attribute - and I will miss some.
I need 'a sales rep can only edit leads assigned to them.' How do you model that?

That is resource-based authorization. I load the lead record, then call IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(user, lead, 'CanEditLead'). The handler receives both the lead and the user, compares lead.AssignedToId to the user's sub claim, and either succeeds or fails. You cannot express this with an attribute alone because the attribute runs before the resource is loaded.
How do you make every endpoint require authentication by default without decorating each one?

Set a default authorization policy in AddAuthorization to RequireAuthenticatedUser. Every endpoint then requires authentication unless explicitly decorated with [AllowAnonymous]. This is the deny-by-default approach and prevents accidentally exposed endpoints when a developer forgets the attribute.
How do you test a custom requirement handler?

I instantiate the handler in a unit test, create an AuthorizationHandlerContext with a controlled ClaimsPrincipal and the resource under test, call HandleAsync, and assert on context.HasSucceeded or context.HasFailed. The handler is a plain class with no HTTP dependencies, so testing it needs no mocking of the middleware stack.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Azure AD / Entra ID and Managed Identity, and how do they help?

answer.oneLiner: Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) is Microsoft's cloud identity provider for user and application sign-in. Managed Identity gives an Azure resource its own Entra identity so it can call other services with no passwords or secrets in code.
answer.detail: Entra ID is Microsoft's implementation of an OIDC and OAuth 2.0 identity provider. It handles user authentication, issues tokens, manages MFA, and integrates with enterprise directories. For applications, it issues tokens for delegated access (on behalf of a user) and application access (using client credentials). Managed Identity solves a specific and common problem: how does your Azure App Service or Azure Function authenticate to Azure SQL, Key Vault, or Blob Storage without storing a password or a service principal secret in config? With a system-assigned Managed Identity, Azure creates an Entra identity that is tied to that specific resource. Azure rotates the underlying credentials automatically. The application calls DefaultAzureCredential, which detects the environment and uses the Managed Identity token when running in Azure. No connection string, no secret, no rotation to manage. You then grant the Managed Identity the appropriate RBAC role on the target resource - for example, 'Key Vault Secrets User' or 'Storage Blob Data Reader'. A user-assigned Managed Identity is standalone - it can be assigned to multiple resources and has its own lifecycle, useful when multiple services share one identity.
keyPoints: Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud IdP for OAuth 2.0 and OIDC - it issues ID tokens and access tokens
Managed Identity gives an Azure resource its own Entra identity - no stored credentials anywhere
Azure rotates the underlying credential automatically - the platform handles key management
Grant the Managed Identity the minimum RBAC role needed on each target resource

code.note: DefaultAzureCredential chains through multiple credential sources - Managed Identity in Azure, developer CLI credentials locally - so the same code works in both environments.

answer.analogy: Managed Identity is your employee badge that the office issues and renews - you never carry your own keys.
redFlags: Storing service principal client secrets in appsettings.json when Managed Identity is available
Not knowing the difference between system-assigned and user-assigned Managed Identity
Thinking Managed Identity only works for Key Vault
Not knowing what DefaultAzureCredential does

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between system-assigned and user-assigned Managed Identity?
followUpsQA.a: System-assigned is tied to one specific resource and is deleted when that resource is deleted. User-assigned is a standalone identity that can be assigned to multiple resources and has an independent lifecycle.

followUpsQA.q: How does Managed Identity remove the secret management problem?
followUpsQA.a: Azure generates and rotates the underlying credentials on your behalf. Your code just calls DefaultAzureCredential, which retrieves a short-lived token from the local IMDS endpoint - no connection string, no rotation script, no leaked secret in config.

followUpsQA.q: What RBAC roles do you grant a Managed Identity?
followUpsQA.a: The minimum needed for the task. For reading secrets: Key Vault Secrets User. For reading blobs: Storage Blob Data Reader. Granting Owner or Contributor is excessive and violates least privilege.

followUpsQA.q: How does the same code work locally when there is no Managed Identity?
followUpsQA.a: DefaultAzureCredential chains through multiple sources. Locally it falls through to Azure CLI credentials or Visual Studio credentials, so developers do not need a local Managed Identity.

followUpsQA.q: What is Workload Identity Federation?
followUpsQA.a: It allows non-Azure workloads - like a GitHub Actions runner or a Kubernetes pod - to authenticate to Entra ID using a token from their own platform without any stored secret, extending the same passwordless principle outside of Azure.

dialogue.text: What are Entra ID and Managed Identity, and why are they useful?

Entra ID, formerly Azure AD, is Microsoft's cloud identity provider - it handles user authentication, issues OAuth 2.0 access tokens and OIDC ID tokens, and manages enterprise identity for apps. Managed Identity solves a different problem: how does your application authenticate to other Azure services without storing a password or secret in config.
Concretely, how does a .NET API use Managed Identity to read from Key Vault?

Enable system-assigned Managed Identity on the App Service. Grant that identity the 'Key Vault Secrets User' role on the Key Vault. In code, call AddAzureKeyVault with new DefaultAzureCredential(). DefaultAzureCredential talks to the local IMDS endpoint in Azure to get a short-lived token - no client ID, no secret, no rotation to manage.
What if multiple App Services need to share the same Key Vault access?

Use a user-assigned Managed Identity. Create it as a standalone Azure resource, assign it to all the App Services that need it, and grant it the Key Vault role once. System-assigned identities are tied to one resource, so they cannot be shared - user-assigned is the right choice when multiple resources share an identity.
A developer asks why they cannot just put the service principal client secret in appsettings. What do you tell them?

Client secrets in config end up in source control, get copied into Docker images, and appear in deployment logs. Every rotation requires a code change and a deployment. Managed Identity means the platform manages the credential - there is nothing to rotate, nothing to leak, and nothing to commit by accident.
Does this pattern work in a local development environment?

Yes - DefaultAzureCredential chains through multiple credential sources. Locally it falls back to Azure CLI credentials or Visual Studio account credentials. The same code that uses Managed Identity in production picks up the developer's CLI login locally with no code change. You just grant the developer's personal Entra account the same Key Vault role for local access.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Give an overview of ASP.NET Core Identity.

answer.oneLiner: ASP.NET Core Identity is the built-in membership system for managing users, passwords, roles, and sign-in. It handles registration, login, password hashing, lockout, 2FA, and external logins, backed by EF Core by default.
answer.detail: ASP.NET Core Identity is a library that ships with the framework and provides a complete local account system. It stores users, roles, claims, and tokens in a relational database via EF Core. Registration flow: Identity creates a user record, hashes the password using PBKDF2 with a random salt, and stores the hash. Login flow: Identity retrieves the hash, runs the same hash on the provided password with the same salt, and compares. If it matches, a sign-in cookie or token is issued. Identity also handles: account lockout after N failed attempts, email confirmation tokens, password reset tokens, two-factor authentication (TOTP or SMS), and external logins (Google, Microsoft, GitHub via OAuth). You interact with it through UserManager, RoleManager, and SignInManager which are DI-registered services. The right time to reach for Identity is for first-party local accounts in a single application. When you need SSO across multiple apps, enterprise federation, or social login at scale, an external identity provider like Entra ID or Auth0 is the better choice.
keyPoints: Handles registration, login, password hashing (PBKDF2 with salt), and lockout out of the box
Supports 2FA (TOTP), email confirmation, password reset, and external logins (OAuth)
Access via DI-registered UserManager, RoleManager, and SignInManager services
Use for first-party local accounts; use an external IdP for SSO, federation, or B2C scale

code.note: UserManager.CreateAsync hashes the password with PBKDF2 and a unique salt - you never touch raw passwords or hashing logic.

answer.analogy: ASP.NET Core Identity is the security desk that already exists - do not build your own when it is already there.
redFlags: Rolling your own password hashing instead of using Identity or a proven library
Using Identity when you actually need SSO across multiple applications
Not knowing what UserManager, RoleManager, and SignInManager do
Thinking Identity handles OAuth/OIDC token issuance - it does not, that is a separate server

followUpsQA.q: How does Identity hash passwords?
followUpsQA.a: It uses PBKDF2 with a random salt generated per user and an adaptive iteration count. The algorithm and parameters are stored alongside the hash so the system can re-hash at login if the iteration count increases.

followUpsQA.q: What is account lockout and how do you configure it?
followUpsQA.a: Identity tracks failed login attempts. After a configurable number of failures (default 5), the account is locked for a configurable duration. Set this in IdentityOptions.Lockout when calling AddIdentity.

followUpsQA.q: When would you choose an external IdP over Identity?
followUpsQA.a: When you need SSO across multiple applications, enterprise federation (Active Directory, SAML), social login at scale, or B2C user management. Identity is for a single app with local accounts.

followUpsQA.q: Does ASP.NET Core Identity issue JWTs or OAuth tokens?
followUpsQA.a: No. Identity is a membership system, not a token server. To issue JWTs or OAuth tokens you need a separate authorization server like Duende IdentityServer or Keycloak, which can use Identity as its user store.

followUpsQA.q: How do you support external logins (Google, GitHub) with Identity?
followUpsQA.a: Call AddAuthentication().AddGoogle() or AddGitHub() with your OAuth app credentials. Identity's SignInManager handles the external login callback and links the external account to a local user record.

dialogue.text: Give me an overview of ASP.NET Core Identity.

It is the built-in membership system for local accounts. It handles the full lifecycle: registration with PBKDF2 password hashing, login with lockout protection, email confirmation, password reset, 2FA, and external logins. It stores everything in a relational database via EF Core and exposes the functionality through DI-registered services like UserManager and SignInManager.
What does UserManager actually give you?

UserManager is the main service for user operations. You call CreateAsync to register a user - it hashes the password internally with PBKDF2 and a unique salt. FindByEmailAsync to look up a user. CheckPasswordAsync to validate credentials. AddToRoleAsync to assign roles. GenerateEmailConfirmationTokenAsync for a confirmation link. You never touch raw passwords or hashing logic yourself.
A startup wants SSO across their three web apps. Should they use Identity?

Not on its own. Identity is a local account store for a single application. For SSO across multiple apps, you need an OAuth 2.0 and OIDC authorization server - something like Duende IdentityServer, Keycloak, or Entra ID. Identity can serve as the user store behind such a server, but the SSO and token issuance layer is separate.
Does Identity issue JWTs?

No. Identity is a membership system, not a token server. It produces sign-in cookies and internal tokens for things like email confirmation and password reset, but not OAuth access tokens or OIDC ID tokens. For that you need a separate authorization server that can use Identity as its user store.
A developer wants to skip Identity and roll their own user store. When might that be justified?

Almost never for the password hashing and account management parts - rolling your own is how you get MD5 passwords in a breach. A legitimate reason is when your user store is an external system like LDAP or a legacy database with a fixed schema. Even then, you would implement IUserStore against that schema rather than bypass Identity's hashing and lockout logic completely.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you revoke JWTs given they're stateless?

answer.oneLiner: You cannot truly invalidate a stateless JWT before it expires. You mitigate with very short access token lifetimes, server-side revocation at the refresh step, and a denylist or token-version check for forced immediate logout.
answer.detail: A stateless JWT is self-contained. The API validates it by checking the signature and claims alone, with no server lookup. This means there is no place to delete a token - the server does not track which tokens are active. The practical approaches are: (1) Keep access tokens short-lived (5-15 minutes). Most logout scenarios are satisfied by not issuing new access tokens at the refresh step, which expires the session within minutes. (2) For forced immediate logout - banned users, compromised accounts, security incidents - maintain a server-side denylist keyed on the JWT's jti (JWT ID) claim. On every request, check the jti against the denylist before processing. This reintroduces a server lookup and some statefulness but is fast with Redis or an in-memory cache. (3) Use a per-user token version or security stamp. Store a version number per user. Include it in the JWT as a claim. On validation, check the token's version matches the stored version. Incrementing the stored version instantly invalidates all existing tokens for that user. (4) Rotating the signing key is a global kill-switch - all existing tokens fail signature validation. Reserve this for emergency scenarios.
keyPoints: You cannot revoke a stateless JWT before expiry without server-side state - that is the fundamental tradeoff
Short access token lifetime (5-15 min) plus revocation at the refresh step covers most logout scenarios
Forced immediate logout: denylist on jti claim in Redis, or per-user token version check
Rotating the signing key invalidates all tokens globally - emergency use only

code.note: The security stamp check adds one DB read per request but gives instant revocation for all tokens issued before the stamp was changed.

answer.analogy: A JWT is like a concert ticket - once printed, valid until the show ends, unless security specifically flags your ticket number at the door.
redFlags: Claiming you can instantly revoke a stateless JWT without any server-side mechanism
Using hour-long or day-long access tokens with no revocation plan
Not knowing what the jti claim is used for
Saying 'just delete the cookie' as if that revokes the JWT itself

followUpsQA.q: What is the jti claim and how is it used in revocation?
followUpsQA.a: jti is the JWT ID - a unique identifier for that specific token. A revocation denylist stores jti values of revoked tokens. On each request you check whether the incoming token's jti is in the denylist and reject it if so.

followUpsQA.q: What is a security stamp and how does it enable revocation?
followUpsQA.a: A per-user value stored in the database that is included as a claim in issued tokens. When you change a user's password or force logout, you regenerate the stamp. Any token issued before the stamp change has the old value and fails the validation check.

followUpsQA.q: What is the tradeoff between a denylist and short expiry?
followUpsQA.a: Short expiry keeps the system stateless but leaves a window where a compromised token is still valid. A denylist gives instant revocation but requires a server-side lookup on every request, re-introducing statefulness.

followUpsQA.q: How do you use the refresh step for revocation?
followUpsQA.a: When a user logs out or is suspended, mark their refresh tokens as revoked. The short-lived access token expires on its own within minutes, and the system stops issuing new ones at the refresh step.

followUpsQA.q: When would you rotate the signing key?
followUpsQA.a: Only in a security emergency - for example, the signing key was compromised. Rotating it invalidates every token for every user across the entire application, which is disruptive but sometimes necessary.

dialogue.text: JWTs are stateless. How do you revoke one?

The honest answer is that you cannot truly revoke a stateless JWT before it expires without adding some server-side mechanism. The mitigation is keeping access tokens very short-lived - 5 to 15 minutes. For most logout scenarios that is enough: you revoke the refresh token so no new access tokens are issued, and the current access token expires naturally.
What if a user account is compromised and you need to kick them out immediately?

Two options. A jti denylist in Redis: store the unique JWT ID of revoked tokens and check it on every request - fast, but adds a cache lookup per call. Or a per-user security stamp: include the stamp as a claim in the JWT and check it against the database on each request. Regenerating the stamp invalidates all tokens for that user instantly.
What is the cost of the security stamp approach?

One database read per request to fetch the current stamp. In a high-traffic API that adds up, so you cache it with a short TTL - say 30 seconds - which means revocation takes effect within that window rather than instantly. It is a tradeoff between strictness and performance.
A developer suggests just deleting the JWT from the client cookie to log out. Is that sufficient?

No. Deleting the cookie is a client-side operation. An attacker who copied the token before logout still has a valid JWT that the server will accept until it expires. True revocation requires the server to reject the token, which is why you need the stamp, denylist, or at least a very short expiry.
Is there a global kill-switch for all tokens in an emergency?

Yes - rotate the signing key. Every existing token was signed with the old key. When the API reloads its keys from the JWKS endpoint, all old signatures fail verification and every token across all users is instantly invalid. It is very disruptive - every active user gets logged out - so it is reserved for a true security emergency like a signing key compromise.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
